papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper from MIT claims that quantum wave functions can be derived exactly from classical particle paths alone — no Feynman "sum over all histories" needed — by combining multi-valued classical action solutions with classical fluid density. The review board found the individual examples correct but the central proof fatally flawed: it silently assumes a condition that does not hold in general, and the paper's treatment of the Bell inequality experiment substitutes continuous spinor math for the discrete binary measurements that Bell's theorem actually governs. All worked examples succeed for reasons already known to physics, not because the general framework is valid.

papers.technical_takeaway: The central proof (Lemma 1) requires the Eulerian spatial gradient of the classical density ρ_k to vanish — which eliminates the Bohm quantum potential Q = −(ℏ²/2m)(∇²√ρ_k / √ρ_k) and recovers the classical Hamilton-Jacobi equation from the Schrödinger equation — but this condition is neither stated as a hypothesis nor generally true. All three reviewers independently identified this as a fatal gap across both review cycles. The paper conflates Lagrangian path density (a scalar along a trajectory, no x-dependence by construction) with Eulerian density field (a function of x and t), and every worked example succeeds because Q = 0 coincidentally holds in that case: spherical-wave 1/r² density in 3D (∇²(1/r) = 0), quadratic Hamiltonians (Feynman-exact by prior work), and Coulomb via the Duru–Kleinert mapping to the harmonic oscillator. No example with a general anharmonic potential is provided. The EPR/Bell section computes correlations of continuous spinor projections before binary quantization — which avoids Bell's theorem rather than refuting it. All relativistic extensions inherit the unestablished base lemma.
papers.title: On computing quantum waves exactly from classical and relativistic action
papers.transcript_video_title: Quantum Physics Is An Unnecessary Complication Physicists Say
